body_txt: A Tech Bro is someone connected with the technology and business industry; cultivating an image of intelligence mixed with money, counterculture attitude and social savvy. As for whether they are actually hip, business literate, anti-establishment, forward-thinking, passionate about electronics, skilled at engineering, or even intelligent in any form , that depends on the individual. Their goal is to charm investors into funding their project, so some degree of false presentation is necessary. Typically they are young men in their twenties and early thirties, insisting on casual clothing, shaggy hair and Perma-Stubble . Some of them are keen on partying and getting up to wacky hijinx , and are easily distracted during business meetings. Their speech is a blend of Technobabble and corporate buzzwords designed to attract investors, self-promoting at every opportunity. Should they achieve wealth , they'll often conspicuously "upgrade" their casual style with prominent brands, buy fancy cars, and attend even fancier parties to court even bigger investors. They see themselves as leaders, even visionaries , and they want you to know it. Despite their similarity to the Stereotypical Nerd (including more casual and less strait-laced Silicon Valley versions), Tech Bros lean hard into stereotypically masculine traits — instead of usually being awkward and socially isolated, they've turned into fratbros (if they aren't just fratbros and hedge fund kids who fell into funding tech startups), media/tech-savvy corporate executives , and suave influencers . Sometimes, they're Jerk Jocks who happen to be able to code and sneer at "pasty nerds" and "betas" , or even Nerdy Bullies or Internet Jerks . While the social awkwardness of a classic nerd may linger, they HAVE to show some degree of charisma that proves them Proud to Be a Geek . With money on the line, they have to be. Mad Scientists working in modern tech sectors are often some form of this trope, with their sheer cunning manipulation (and possibly literal madness ) carrying over. Because of this, the Tech Bro is also often a far darker figure than the traditional nerd: a glib malignant narcissist whose people skills lie in manipulation rather than empathy. They might not even be interested in programming, electronics, product design, software architecture, or even their product itself. They're interested in monetizing tech. They want to found the next big tech company, and they are frankly at home in the corporate world after looking past the nonstandard English and casual clothes. They'll talk about stock options and capturing market share as much as they do about research and coding. They might be a Rich Gadgeteer Genius , or they might just be a manager of geniuses. Their marketing department will tell you how they're improving the world by disrupting the status quo, and they tend to think that "outdated" rules shouldn't be allowed to constrain their new business model. Politically, there is a duality here; some are progressive idealists who genuinely believe that their technology will save the world , while others just want to be unaccountable potentates dictating the course of the world from ivory towers . And some claim to be the former , but funnel their money towards the latter . They have their own style, too. A classic nerd is generally considered uncool, unfashionable or even literally unhygienic, but if a Tech Bro has any trace of a classic nerd's unkemptness, it's only a carefully cultivated fashion statement. They pay a lot of attention to their clothing and personal grooming, though they usually shirk the sharp suits of their Yuppie precursors for a more youthful, business-casual look (often with "casual" clothing that is far more expensive than any normal person could afford, like a 500-dollar shirt that looks equivalent to one you can buy in bulk for 4 dollars apiece). Also, while classic nerds are usually either overweight or scrawny weaklings, a Tech Bro is often in good physical shape, perhaps aided by fancy health-tracking gadgets, personal trainers, and faddish diet regimes. They're more likely to live in stylish, airy, bright houses rather than in dark basements full of old fast food containers or converted warehouses full of electronic equipment, and their office is either the same stark, monochrome minimalist style of interior design or a Wacky Startup Workplace (bonus points for being located in real-life tech hubs like Silicon Valley). If there is a Mad Science Fair , expect Tech Bros to show up often. Don't expect them to work on any side projects they don't plan on monetizing. With respect to the work hard play hard ethic, this often translates into attempts to be a ladies man with varying degrees of success. In many cases, despite their attempts to work out and have fit bodies, they still may fit into the Kavorka Man category due to it being obvious that unlike naturally handsome men, they have to put in a lot of work on their appearance . The wise tech bro realizes that it's time to settle down after they reach around age 35 as their attempts were creepy enough when they were young men; it only gets creepier when they're older... The Tech Bro archetype originated from the mythos surrounding the head honchos of early personal computer companies, including Steve Jobs, Bill Gates, Steve Wozniak, and Nolan Bushnell. This archetype arguably began to separate from the general "geek" one with the " Dot Com Bubble " that imploded around 2000 and the aftershock of internet-focused businesses like Google and Facebook — the founders of such companies are often models for tech bro characters in media, to the point where you can sometimes tell which specific person (or indeed, persons ) a character is inspired by. A specific sub-variant of this character primarily emerging from The New '20s is the "Crypto Bro" (not to be confused with cryptography or encryption experts), who (at least allegedly) made his fortune from speculating on Bitcoin, its fellow cryptocurrencies, and/or NFTs . Very similar to a Tech Bro, with a tendency to enjoy flashy spending and be even more egotistical than usual. Has a better-than-average chance of turning out to be a Con Man whose "digital assets" business is a massive pyramid scheme , especially if — as is so often the case — they have no technical background whatsoever and just jumped on the crypto bandwagon to create a "Meme Coin" which is doomed to fail (though hopefully not before the Crypto Bro is able to convert his fifteen minutes of fame into cash). The phrase " x Bro" is also swiftly becoming shorthand for someone in a field who could sell ice to the Inuit, note or should that be 'crypto to a merchant banker'? but anything they do seems quite sketchy. As cryptocurrency started to go out of style, this stereotype became replaced with or at least paralleled by the "A.I. Bro", who tends to treat A.I.-Generated Artwork as a replacement for human-made art and try to push "A.I." technology or have such tech automate aspects of their lives, with one byproduct of this being the practice of "vibe coding", or using A.I. to automatically program software, often without bothering to check the output to make sure it works ; many jokes abound about how such technology could turn against humanity or take over their lives . Of note, "techbro" is often applied to plain-ol' socially-awkward nerds working in tech, especially rich engineers and coders; however, this trope focuses more on aforementioned masculine and charismatic qualities given to tech moguls or tech executives, or engineers/coders with actual social skills (or at least the appearance of such). The latter variant though do exist as a heroic, sympathetic variant, in which a tech-focused Science Hero and Playful Hacker will end up as they get older or employed. Compare with Yuppie (an older, more formal portrayal of young and ambitious white-collar workers), Playful Hacker and The Cracker (for another portrayal of glamorized computer geeks) as well as Misfit Lab Rat (another scientific-oriented subculture with similar clothing styles, albeit more unkempt and more rebellious). A tech bro who works in social media may be used for a Social Media Is Bad aesop. Pairs of these have a tendency to form a Technician/Performer Team-Up in both fiction and reality, often with one being a skilled-but-awkward programmer and another a charismatic-but-untechnical "face". The genderswapped version is often a Girlboss Feminist who's just as bad as the boys, if not openly undermining women on behalf of them. She may be criticized even by therapists for being unfeminine. Because this trope often has negative connotations, No Real Life Examples, Please!
